content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Patrick Tang 
Tehachapi City Attorney 
Jarvis, Fay, Doporto & Gibson, LLP 
492 Ninth Street, suite 310 
Oakland, CA 94607 
May 23, 2017 
Re: 
Your Request for lnfonnal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-17-085 
Dear Mr. Tang: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 Because your question is general in nature and not limited to 
specific governmental proceedings, we are treating your request as one for infonnal assistancc.2 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
May a public official who resides within a landscape and lighting assessment district take 
part in decisions to adjust the assessment rate for the district, including preliminary determinations 
regarding a resolution describing the proposed improvements or substantial changes and ordering 
an engineer's report? 
CONCLUSION 
So long as the decision adjusts the rate for all residences in the district equally, 
proportionally, or by the same percentage, an official who resides in an assessment district may take 
part in decisions to adjust the existing assessment rate under the public generally exception for 
broadly provided public services in Regulation I 8703(c)( I ).3 
1 The Political Reform Act 1s contained in Go\'emment Code Sections 81000 through 9101-l. All statutory 
references are to the Go\·emment Code. unless otherwise md1cated. The regulauons of the Fair Poliucal Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 181 JO through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulauons. All 
regulatory references are to Tille 2. Di\·ision 6 of the Califom[a Code of Regulations, unless otherwise mdicated. 
Æ lnfonnal assistance does not pro\'ide the requestor \\ 1th the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
wrillen advice. (Section 1!3114; Regulation I 8329(c)(3).) 


FACTS 
File No. 1-17-085 
Page No. 2 
The California Landscaping and Lighting Act of 1972 allows local government agencies to 
fonn assessment districts for the purpose of financing the costs and expenses of landscaping and 
lighting public places. To fonn an assessment district, local agencies must ( l) adopt a resolution 
describing any proposed new improvements or substantial changes in existing improvements and 
ordering the engineer to prepare a report and (2) approve the engineer's report and adopt a 
resolution of intention. The resolution of intention declares the City Council's intention to levy and 
collect assessments within the assessment district for the fiscal year. It also states whether the 
assessment is proposed to change from the previous year and sets a date, time, and place for a 
public hearing. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making. or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. A public official has a "financial interest'' in a governmental decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public 
official's interests set forth in Section 87103. As pertinent to this request, the only interest 
implicated by your account of the facts is the official's interest in his or her residence located within 
an assessment district. Under Section 87103(b), an official has an interest in any real property in 
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. Thus, we must detennine if a 
decision to adjust an assessment will have a foreseeable and material effect on the official's 
property interest. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's interest is presumed to be 
reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. Regulation 
18701(a) provides: 
"Financial Interest Explicitly Involved: A financial effect on a financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named 
party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the 
official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, pennit, 
or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any 
governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in 
Regulation l 8702.2(a)( l )-(6)." 
Additionally. Regulation I 8702.2(a)(3) provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on real property is material whenever the governmental decision 
''[w]ould impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel." 
1 We note that our conclusion is limited to the adjustment of an existing assessment. We do not addrc:-s the 
establishment of a new landscape and lighting assessment diÇtrict or changes 10 the boundaries of a district. 


File No. I-17-085 
Page No. 3 
Accordingly, the financial effect of a decision to adjust an assessment, which applies to an 
official's residence, will have a foreseeable and material effect on the official's property interest 
and, absent an applicable exception, the official will have a disqualifying financial interest in the 
decision. 
Public General(v Exception 
Section 87103 prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the 
decision on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the general rule to detennine if the effect of the decision on the 
official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official 
establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or 
her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant 
segment." 
In most cases, a "significant segment of the public" is defined as at least 25 percent of (I) 
the businesses or non-profit entities; (2) the real property, commercial real property, or residential 
real property; or (3) the individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation I 8703(b).) While 
you have not indicated whether the decision in question will affect at least 25 percent the residential 
real property within the city, Regulation I 8703(e) provides additional limited exceptions, which 
apply in special circumstances. As pertinent to your question, Regulation l 8703(e)( I )  provides an 
exception if the official establishes: 
"The decision establishes or adjusts assessments, taxes, fees, or rates for 
water, utility, or other broadly provided public services or facilities that are applied 
equally, proportionally, or by the same percentage to the official's interest and other 
businesses. properties, or individuals subject to the assessment, tax, fee, or rate." 
An assessment under the California Landscaping and Lighting Act is for broadly provided 
public services. So long as the adjustment applies equally, proportionally, or by the same 
percentage to all other residences within the district and barring any other interest, an official who 
resides in an assessment district may take part in decisions to adjust the existing assessment, 
including preliminary detenninations regarding a resolution describing the proposed improvements 
or substantial changes and ordering an engineer's report, under Regulation 18703( e)( I ). 


File No. 1-17-085 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
